flags.stem_snippet: 4-month-old girl, first febrile UTI, E. coli, defervesced 36h. Bladder US during admission shown.
flags.issue: Correct option mandates DMSA scan at 4-6 months after a FIRST UTI in an infant whose ultrasound was already done. RCH Melbourne CPG does not include routine DMSA in the post-UTI imaging cascade; DMSA is a NICE construct. RCH says renal US during illness for atypical UTI only, and 'most children do not require an ultrasound for a first UTI'.
flags.rch_says: Non-urgent renal US for recurrent UTIs only; US during acute illness for atypical UTI; infants <6 months with recurrent UTI or no antenatal scans get US. DMSA/MCUG not part of RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Rewrite correct option to: 'No additional imaging is required after the normal renal tract ultrasound; counsel parents on prompt presentation for any future febrile illness.' Demote DMSA option to a NICE-influenced distractor with a rationale noting RCH does not mandate DMSA.

flags.stem_snippet: 2-year-old, second febrile E. coli UTI in 6 months, prior US normal at first UTI.
flags.issue: Correct option is 'DMSA scan in 4-6 months to assess for cortical scarring'. RCH does not mandate DMSA. RCH would call this recurrent UTI and recommend a renal US (already done normal at first UTI; repeat US in the recurrent setting is the RCH path). Sources cite NICE NG224 alongside RCH, but RCH is meant to be the source-of-truth.
flags.rch_says: Recurrent UTI imaging = renal US; specialist referral; no routine DMSA in RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Rewrite correct option to: 'Repeat renal tract ultrasound and paediatric nephrology referral for recurrent UTI; no routine DMSA.' Move DMSA into a NICE-aligned distractor.

flags.stem_snippet: 3-year-old, second culture-proven E. coli febrile UTI; first 4 months ago.
flags.issue: Correct option specifies 'DMSA scan 4-6 months after most recent UTI'. RCH guideline does not include DMSA in the routine recurrent-UTI cascade.
flags.rch_says: Recurrent UTI = non-urgent renal US plus specialist consideration; DMSA not in RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Replace correct option with 'Non-urgent renal tract ultrasound and paediatric nephrology referral for recurrent UTI.' Note in rationale that DMSA is a NICE recommendation not adopted by RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 3-year-old, third culture-confirmed E. coli UTI in 12 months.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'DMSA renal scan in 4-6 months'. Sources cite RCH but also NICE and CARI; correct option follows NICE not RCH.
flags.rch_says: RCH recommends renal US for recurrent UTI; DMSA/MCUG not part of the published RCH cascade.
flags.proposed_fix: Rewrite to 'Renal tract ultrasound and paediatric nephrology referral'. Demote DMSA option.

flags.stem_snippet: 8-month-old, first febrile UTI, normal renal US, completing 10-day course on oral cephalexin.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months'. After a single typical UTI with normal US, RCH explicitly states 'most children do not require an ultrasound for a first UTI' and DMSA is not in the RCH cascade.
flags.rch_says: First typical UTI with normal US: no further imaging routinely required. DMSA not in RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Rewrite correct option to 'No additional imaging required; parental education on prompt presentation for future fevers.' Move DMSA to a NICE-influenced distractor.

flags.stem_snippet: 5-month-old, first febrile UTI, antenatal scans normal.
flags.issue: Correct option arranges renal US during admission for a TYPICAL first UTI in a 5-month-old with normal antenatal scans. Stem doesn't establish atypical features; RCH does not mandate US during admission for typical first UTI in this age group when antenatal scans are normal.
flags.rch_says: Renal US during acute illness reserved for atypical UTI. Infants <6 months without antenatal monitoring or with recurrent UTI: US. With normal antenatal scans and typical first UTI: no routine US.
flags.proposed_fix: Clarify in stem whether antenatal monitoring was complete and whether atypical features are present, then make correct option 'no further imaging' if typical or 'renal US' if atypical/no antenatal scans. As written it overstates US indication.

flags.stem_snippet: 6-month-old girl, first febrile UTI, E. coli, responded to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'Renal tract ultrasound within 2 weeks'. After a typical first UTI in a 6-month-old with no atypical features and normal response, RCH does not routinely require US. Stem doesn't establish atypical UTI or absent antenatal monitoring.
flags.rch_says: Most children do not require US after first UTI; US reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI, or <6 months with no antenatal monitoring or recurrent infection.
flags.proposed_fix: Either add atypical feature or absent antenatal monitoring to justify US, or change correct option to 'no routine imaging'. Also note empiric amoxicillin-clavulanate is not the RCH first-line; RCH lists cefalexin (cystitis) or gentamicin (IV).

flags.stem_snippet: 9-month-old, day 6 fever despite ceftriaxone, conjunctival injection, cracked lips - Kawasaki.
flags.issue: Not a UTI Q (Kawasaki) - swept into UTI bucket by keyword 'urine cultures sterile'. No UTI flag; mention only for triage accuracy.
flags.rch_says: N/A - Kawasaki management with IVIG + aspirin is RCH-aligned.
flags.proposed_fix: No fix needed; flagged as false-positive topic classification.

flags.stem_snippet: 14-month-old non-Aboriginal girl, 2 days fever, irritability, clean catch urine collected; await culture.
flags.issue: Correct option starts oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole empirically. RCH first-line empirical oral for paediatric UTI is cefalexin, not trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Trimethoprim is acceptable when sensitivities known but is not the RCH empirical recommendation.
flags.rch_says: Empiric oral cystitis: cefalexin 20 mg/kg (max 750 mg) TDS. Empiric oral pyelonephritis: cefalexin 25 mg/kg QID (<12 mo) or 45 mg/kg TDS (>=12 mo). Trimethoprim only after sensitivities.
flags.proposed_fix: Change correct option to 'Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose TDS for 7 days while awaiting culture; review at end of course'. Demote trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole to a distractor noting it is reasonable only once sensitivities return.

flags.stem_snippet: 18-month-old, fever, leucocytes+nitrites+, 200 WCC/uL, choosing empiric oral antibiotic.
flags.issue: Correct option 'cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose TWICE daily for 7 days'. RCH cefalexin dosing for pyelonephritis in >=12 months is 45 mg/kg TDS; for cystitis 20 mg/kg TDS. Twice-daily cefalexin is not an RCH-aligned regimen.
flags.rch_says: Cefalexin TDS for cystitis (20 mg/kg) or TDS/QID for pyelonephritis (25 mg/kg QID <12mo; 45 mg/kg TDS >=12mo).
flags.proposed_fix: Change dosing frequency to TDS (three-times daily) per RCH. Choose dose per cystitis vs pyelonephritis framing.

flags.stem_snippet: 4-month-old, fever, catheter urine positive nitrites/leucocytes, choosing IV regimen.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'IV gentamicin 7 mg/kg daily and IV benzylpenicillin'. RCH empirical IV for paediatric UTI is gentamicin alone (with benzylpenicillin/amoxicillin added only if <3 months sepsis cover or listeria/enterococcus risk). At 4 months out of the <3mo bracket the routine RCH regimen is gentamicin monotherapy. Benzylpenicillin gives no UTI cover.
flags.rch_says: IV: gentamicin empirically; amoxicillin/benzylpenicillin only added in <3 months for listeria/enterococcus cover, not for UTI coverage itself.
flags.proposed_fix: If retaining combination regimen at 4 months, justify in rationale; otherwise simplify to 'IV gentamicin pending sensitivities'.

flags.stem_snippet: 5-month-old, fever 39, vomiting, choosing IV empirical regimen.
flags.issue: Same as ctb-p42-003: correct option adds benzylpenicillin/ampicillin to gentamicin at 5 months. RCH IV regimen for paediatric UTI >3 months is gentamicin monotherapy.
flags.rch_says: IV gentamicin alone for UTI >3 months; add ampicillin/benzylpenicillin only for sepsis cover in neonates and young infants.
flags.proposed_fix: Simplify correct option to gentamicin monotherapy at this age, or reframe stem as septic-pathway-not-UTI-only.

flags.stem_snippet: 4-month-old, July, 3 days coryza, increased work of breathing, half feeds.
flags.issue: Correct option targets SpO2 'at least 92%'. RCH Bronchiolitis CPG explicitly targets 90% (not 92%); brief desats <90% alone not an indication to start oxygen. The 92% target is from older NICE/AAP and is more aggressive than RCH.
flags.rch_says: Target SpO2 is 90% unless underlying cardiopulmonary disease. Brief desaturations <90% alone are not an indication to start supplemental oxygen.
flags.proposed_fix: Change SpO2 target from 92% to 90% per RCH. Distinguish in rationale that 92% is NICE/older AAP, not RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 8-month-old, 3 days coryza, wheeze, 60% feeds.
flags.issue: Correct option targets SpO2 92-96% for bronchiolitis. RCH targets 90%; the 92-96% band is a NICE/UK frame.
flags.rch_says: Target SpO2 90%; brief dips below 90% alone do not warrant oxygen.
flags.proposed_fix: Change SpO2 target to 'maintain SpO2 at least 90%' per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 6-week-old, 3 days coryza, sweaty during feeds, low-flow NP O2.
flags.issue: SpO2 target '92 percent or above'. RCH = 90%.
flags.rch_says: Target SpO2 90% in bronchiolitis.
flags.proposed_fix: Change target from 92% to 90% per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 10-month-old, mild bronchiolitis, deciding disposition.
flags.issue: Correct option uses '92% on room air' as admission threshold. RCH would use 90% target. The 92% threshold over-admits per RCH and is NICE-influenced.
flags.rch_says: RCH admission criteria are clinical (apnoea, prematurity <37/40, age <10 weeks, comorbidity, indigenous ethnicity, growth restriction, feeding <50%) plus inability to maintain SpO2 90% on room air, not 92%.
flags.proposed_fix: Change SpO2 admit threshold from 92% to 90% per RCH and add the RCH risk factors as alternative admit triggers.

flags.stem_snippet: 7-week-old, day 4 coryza, 40% feeds, 2 brief apnoeas.
flags.issue: Correct option commences HFNC at 2 L/kg/min directly. RCH explicitly reserves HFNC for failure of standard low-flow oxygen after 2 hours with persistent SpO2 <90%. Stem does not establish prior low-flow trial. HFNC-as-first-line bypasses the RCH ladder.
flags.rch_says: HFNC reserved for failure of standard nasal prong oxygen, defined as SpO2 persistently <90% after 2 hours of low-flow oxygen with no respiratory improvement.
flags.proposed_fix: Either modify stem to establish failed low-flow trial (1-2 L/min for >=2 hr, persistent SpO2 <90%), or change correct option to 'standard low-flow nasal prong oxygen with NG feeds and reassessment'.

flags.stem_snippet: 4-month-old, day 3 coryza, 40% feeds.
flags.issue: Correct option commences humidified HFNC at 2 L/kg/min as first-line respiratory support. Stem does not establish prior low-flow failure. Per RCH, HFNC is reserved for low-flow failure.
flags.rch_says: HFNC = second-line; only after >=2 hours low-flow oxygen with persistent SpO2 <90% and no improvement.
flags.proposed_fix: Reframe correct option as 'low-flow nasal prong oxygen and NG feeds; escalate to HFNC only if low-flow fails after 2 hours', or add a clear failed-low-flow stem element.

flags.stem_snippet: 9-month-old, 3 days coryza, 24 hr increased work of breathing.
flags.issue: Correct option = HFNC at 2 L/kg/min as 'single best initial respiratory intervention'. RCH = low-flow first.
flags.rch_says: Initial respiratory support in bronchiolitis is standard low-flow nasal prong oxygen if SpO2 <90%; HFNC reserved for low-flow failure.
flags.proposed_fix: Change correct option to standard low-flow O2 as initial intervention; move HFNC into a distractor or sequel.

flags.stem_snippet: 4-month-old, 3-day coryza, 50% feeds, no wet nappy 8 hr.
flags.issue: Correct option admits for 'nasal high-flow oxygen'. RCH would use low-flow first; HFNC reserved for low-flow failure. Stem doesn't establish low-flow trial.
flags.rch_says: Standard low-flow nasal prong O2 first-line; HFNC reserved for low-flow failure.
flags.proposed_fix: Change to 'low-flow nasal prong O2 with NG feeds and observation', or add a failed-low-flow precondition to the stem.

flags.stem_snippet: 10-week-old, 3 days coryza, feeding only every 5 hours.
flags.issue: Correct option targets SpO2 'above 92%'. RCH target = 90%.
flags.rch_says: Target SpO2 90% in uncomplicated bronchiolitis.
flags.proposed_fix: Change SpO2 target from 92% to 90% per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 8-month-old, 3 days coryza, 50% feeds.
flags.issue: Correct option supplies 'supplemental oxygen as needed' which is acceptable but the vague phrasing doesn't lock in the RCH 90% target. Minor compared with other Qs.
flags.rch_says: Use SpO2 90% as oxygen titration target.
flags.proposed_fix: Add 'titrate to maintain SpO2 at least 90% per RCH CPG' to lock the target.

flags.stem_snippet: 7-week-old, 4 days coryza, refused feeds 8hr, 3 short apnoeas on low-flow.
flags.issue: Correct option escalates to NIV with PEEP 6 + PICU. RCH does not specifically mandate NIV with named PEEP; HFNC failure then intubation/PICU is the typical RCH-Australian path. NIV intermediate step is more BTS/NICE.
flags.rch_says: After HFNC failure escalate to PICU for intubation; NIV not specifically endorsed as routine in RCH CPG.
flags.proposed_fix: Soften correct option to 'escalate to PICU for advanced respiratory support (NIV or intubation per local PICU)' rather than naming PEEP 6 specifically.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, 12hr barking cough, stridor at rest, oral dex given 30 min ago, persisting stridor.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'Nebulised adrenaline 0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000 solution'. This is the older AAP/AAFP per-kg formula. RCH Melbourne uses a flat dose: 5 mL of 1:1000 (for severe/life-threatening croup) regardless of weight. Per-kg dosing in a small child would underdose vs RCH; in a larger child would overshoot the flat 5 mL ceiling.
flags.rch_says: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 (flat dose) for severe/life-threatening croup.
flags.proposed_fix: Change correct option to 'Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 (flat dose) with concurrent oxygen and minimum 3-hour observation per RCH CPG'. Note in rationale that per-kg dosing is the AAP formula and is not the RCH practice.

flags.stem_snippet: 3yo with mild prior croup, Westley 4 (moderate); asks min observation post last adrenaline.
flags.issue: Correct option states 'Minimum 4 hours after the LAST adrenaline'. RCH CPG explicitly states 'A period of observation of 3 hours is required after nebulised adrenaline'. 4 hours is more conservative than RCH and contradicts the explicit RCH wording the Q purports to test.
flags.rch_says: Observation of 3 hours after nebulised adrenaline is required. Consider extending only for overnight presentations, limited access to care, recurrent stridor, or risk factors.
flags.proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' as the RCH minimum, with caveat about extending for risk factors. Keep clinical-stability criteria.

flags.stem_snippet: 16-month-old, moderate croup, stridor at rest, moderate recession.
flags.issue: Correct option says 'observe for at least 4 hours' after nebulised adrenaline. RCH = 3 hours.
flags.rch_says: 3-hour observation after nebulised adrenaline.
flags.proposed_fix: Change to '3 hours' per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, stridor at rest, distressed; immediate treatment.
flags.issue: 'Minimum 4 hour observation' post nebulised adrenaline. RCH = 3 hours.
flags.rch_says: 3-hour post-adrenaline observation.
flags.proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 3yo, biphasic stridor at rest, sternal recession.
flags.issue: 'Observe for at least 4 hours' post-adrenaline. RCH = 3 hours.
flags.rch_says: 3-hour observation post nebulised adrenaline.
flags.proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 18-month-old, stridor at rest, settled on father's lap. Oral dex + 4-hr obs.
flags.issue: 'Observe for 4 hours before discharge' after oral dexamethasone (no adrenaline given). RCH does not require a fixed 4-hour observation after dexamethasone alone; the 3-hour rule applies after nebulised adrenaline only.
flags.rch_says: 3-hour observation rule applies after nebulised adrenaline. Post-dexamethasone observation should be 'until improvement' (peak dex effect ~1-2 hours).
flags.proposed_fix: Reframe: 'observe until stridor at rest settles and adequate oral intake demonstrated (typically 1-2 hours post-dex)'. 4 hours is over-conservative without adrenaline given.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, stridor at rest, mild intercostal recession, interactive.
flags.issue: Observation 4 hours after oral dex alone (no adrenaline). RCH 3-hour rule applies post-adrenaline only; 4 hr post-dex alone is over-conservative.
flags.rch_says: Post-dexamethasone alone, observe until clinical improvement; 3-hour rule is post-adrenaline.
flags.proposed_fix: Reduce explicit duration from 4 hours; tie to clinical resolution of stridor at rest.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, biphasic stridor at rest, mild intercostal recession, alert.
flags.issue: Correct option = oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg + 'observe in ED for 4 hours' (no adrenaline given). RCH does not require fixed 4-hour observation post-dex alone. Also 0.6 mg/kg is the RCH severe-croup dose; for this stem (mild-moderate features) RCH recommends 0.15 mg/kg.
flags.rch_says: Dex 0.15 mg/kg for mild/moderate; 0.6 mg/kg only for severe/life-threatening. Post-dex observation tied to clinical resolution, not a fixed 4-hour clock.
flags.proposed_fix: Change dexamethasone dose to 0.15 mg/kg (or relabel stem severity as severe to match 0.6 mg/kg). Reduce 4-hour fixed wait to clinical-resolution criterion.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, mild intermittent stridor at rest, mild recession on excitement, settles in mother's arms.
flags.issue: Correct option = oral dex 0.6 mg/kg + 4 hours observation. Stem describes mild croup (intermittent stridor at rest, settles when held). RCH dex dose for mild/moderate = 0.15 mg/kg, not 0.6 mg/kg. Observation 4 hours after dex alone is also over-conservative.
flags.rch_says: Dex 0.15 mg/kg for mild/moderate croup; 0.6 mg/kg reserved for severe/life-threatening.
flags.proposed_fix: Change dose to 0.15 mg/kg per RCH for the moderate severity described, and lose the fixed 4-hour wait.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, barking cough, hoarse cry, audible stridor, increasing WoB - severe croup pathway.
flags.issue: 'Minimum 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose' - RCH says 3 hours.
flags.rch_says: 3-hour observation post nebulised adrenaline.
flags.proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, 4 hours barking cough, noisy breathing, alert; initial pharm management.
flags.issue: Correct option administers BOTH oral dex 0.6 mg/kg AND nebulised adrenaline. Stem describes a child who is 'alert, drinking small sips, pinker after being upset' - this reads as moderate not severe. RCH reserves combined dex 0.6 mg/kg + nebulised adrenaline for severe/life-threatening croup, and 0.15 mg/kg dex alone for mild/moderate.
flags.rch_says: Mild/moderate: dex 0.15 mg/kg PO alone. Severe/life-threatening: dex 0.6 mg/kg (max 12 mg) plus nebulised adrenaline.
flags.proposed_fix: Either upgrade the stem severity (stridor at rest, persistent recessions) to justify 0.6 mg/kg + adrenaline, or change correct option to 'oral dex 0.15 mg/kg alone'.

flags.stem_snippet: 18-month-old, stridor at rest but comfortable in mother's lap.
flags.issue: Correct option observes 'at least 30 minutes' post oral dexamethasone. Dexamethasone peak effect is around 1-2 hours; 30 minutes is too short to demonstrate response. If stridor at rest persists, observation should continue until that settles.
flags.rch_says: Observation should extend until clinical improvement in stridor at rest; dexamethasone peak effect 1-2 hours, not 30 minutes.
flags.proposed_fix: Change '30 minutes' to 'observe until stridor at rest settles (typically 1-2 hours)'.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, barking cough + stridor wakes from sleep; management.
flags.issue: Correct option observes 'at least 30 minutes' before discharge. Inadequate for stridor-at-rest croup; dex effect peaks at 1-2 hours.
flags.rch_says: Observe until stridor at rest settles; dex effect 1-2 hours.
flags.proposed_fix: Extend observation to clinical resolution of stridor at rest (~1-2 hours).

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, intermittent stridor at rest, alert; local dexamethasone supply note.
flags.issue: 'Reassess in 30 minutes' after oral dex. Dex peak action is 1-2 hours; 30 minutes is too early to judge response.
flags.rch_says: Allow 1-2 hours for dexamethasone effect before deciding next steps.
flags.proposed_fix: Change reassessment interval to 1 hour.

flags.stem_snippet: 22-month-old, barking cough, alert, consolable; mild croup.
flags.issue: Observation 'at least 2 hours' after oral dex 0.15 mg/kg. Reasonable but slightly short; RCH suggests waiting for clinical improvement, peak dex effect 1-2 hours. Borderline.
flags.rch_says: Observe until stridor at rest settled; allow 1-2 hours for dexamethasone effect.
flags.proposed_fix: Acceptable as-is for mild croup without stridor at rest; otherwise lengthen to clinical resolution.

flags.stem_snippet: 22-month-old, severe croup, given dex 0.15 mg/kg + nebulised adrenaline; disposition.
flags.issue: Correct option observes '3 hours after nebulised adrenaline'. This is RCH-aligned; however the dex dose 0.15 mg/kg is for mild/moderate; severe-life-threatening croup that warranted nebulised adrenaline should be 0.6 mg/kg per RCH. Stem internal inconsistency, not a correctness flag on the disposition itself.
flags.rch_says: Severe/life-threatening croup: dex 0.6 mg/kg (max 12 mg). 3-hour observation post-adrenaline.
flags.proposed_fix: Either upgrade the dex dose in the stem to 0.6 mg/kg (consistent with severity requiring adrenaline), or soften severity to moderate. Current answer choice (3-hour wait) is correct.

flags.stem_snippet: 2yo, severe croup; dex 8 mg + nebulised adrenaline 5 mL.
flags.issue: Correct option specifies 'observe at least 4 hours'. RCH = 3 hours post-adrenaline. The 8 mg flat dose is reasonable for a ~12-13 kg 2-year-old at 0.6 mg/kg (~7-8 mg); within RCH range.
flags.rch_says: 3-hour observation post-adrenaline.
flags.proposed_fix: Change '4 hours' to '3 hours' per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 5yo, 22 kg, anaphylaxis recovering, prescribing adrenaline autoinjector at discharge.
flags.issue: Correct option prescribes EpiPen Jr 150 mcg for a 22 kg 5-year-old. RCH/ASCIA threshold: 150 mcg autoinjector for 10-20 kg; 300 mcg for >=20 kg. At 22 kg the child should be prescribed the 300 mcg device, not the Jr 150 mcg.
flags.rch_says: Weight 10-20 kg = EpiPen Jr 150 mcg; weight >=20 kg = EpiPen 300 mcg. RCH adrenaline table explicitly lists 5-10 years (30 kg) = EpiPen 300 mcg.
flags.proposed_fix: Change correct option to 'Adrenaline autoinjector 300 mcg (EpiPen) with action plan and training - patient is 22 kg (>=20 kg threshold).'

flags.stem_snippet: 10yo on low-dose ICS, poorly controlled; next step in controller.
flags.issue: Correct option = step up to ICS-LABA (fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50). Australian Asthma Handbook (the AU source-of-truth for chronic asthma; RCH defers to AAH for chronic management) since 2019 recommends ICS-formoterol (single maintenance and reliever therapy, SMART) as preferred step-up in age >=12. For a 10yo it is borderline; fluticasone-salmeterol is acceptable but not the AAH-preferred regimen.
flags.rch_says: RCH Asthma_acute focuses on exacerbations and defers to Australian Asthma Handbook for chronic stepping. AAH prefers ICS-formoterol SMART >=12; for 6-11 years, increase ICS dose or add LABA (salmeterol or formoterol).
flags.proposed_fix: Acceptable as-is at age 10; consider noting in rationale that ICS-formoterol becomes preferred at age 12.

flags.stem_snippet: 7yo on fluticasone 50 BD, poor control, next preventer step.
flags.issue: Correct option doubles ICS dose. Reasonable per AAH 6-11 step-up. No RCH contradiction.
flags.rch_says: AAH 6-11 years: optimise low-dose ICS then either increase ICS or add LABA. Either acceptable.
flags.proposed_fix: No fix needed - documented for completeness.

flags.stem_snippet: 9yo on budesonide-formoterol SMART, well-controlled 12 months, step-down.
flags.issue: Correct option steps down dose. AAH endorses step-down after >=3 months of control. RCH-aligned.
flags.rch_says: AAH supports step-down after 3 months stable control.
flags.proposed_fix: No fix needed.

flags.stem_snippet: 6yo 22 kg, peanut anaphylaxis after cetirizine; next dose adrenaline.
flags.issue: Correct option repeats IM adrenaline at 'IM 220 microg' (10 mcg/kg). At 22 kg the RCH banded table gives 5-10 years (30 kg) = 0.3 mL = 300 mcg. The per-kg ANZCOR figure of 220 mcg under-doses vs RCH band.
flags.rch_says: RCH table: 5-10 years (30 kg) = 0.3 mL of 1:1000 = 300 mcg; >=10 kg threshold for EpiPen Jr 150 mcg, >=20 kg threshold for EpiPen 300 mcg.
flags.proposed_fix: Change repeat IM adrenaline dose to 300 mcg (0.3 mL of 1:1000) per RCH band for 5-10 years. Update saline bolus to '20 mL/kg = 440 mL' (already approximately correct).

flags.stem_snippet: 6yo, cashew anaphylaxis, lip swelling, urticaria, hoarse voice.
flags.issue: Correct option states 'observe for at least 4 hours AFTER SYMPTOMS RESOLVE'. RCH specifies '4 hours from last adrenaline dose OR symptom onset' (which RCH operationalises as last adrenaline dose). 'After symptoms resolve' is longer than RCH minimum and not the RCH wording.
flags.rch_says: All children with anaphylaxis observed for at least 4 hours from last adrenaline dose (or symptom onset).
flags.proposed_fix: Change observation anchor to '4 hours from last adrenaline dose' per RCH.

flags.stem_snippet: 5yo, peanut anaphylaxis, EpiPen Jr already given 5 min ago, continued wheeze.
flags.issue: Correct option uses per-kg formula 'IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg'. RCH uses an age-band table for paediatric anaphylaxis with specified mL of 1:1000. Per-kg dosing here is acceptable per ANZCOR but is not the RCH banded approach.
flags.rch_says: RCH paediatric IM adrenaline dosing uses banded age/weight table; 5-10 years (30 kg) = 0.3 mL of 1:1000 = 300 mcg.
flags.proposed_fix: Replace per-kg phrasing with RCH banded dose (300 mcg = 0.3 mL of 1:1000 for a 5-year-old).

flags.stem_snippet: 12yo, refractory anaphylaxis after 2 IM doses, fluid 60 mL/kg, adrenaline infusion already running.
flags.issue: Correct option commences IV vasopressin infusion. RCH refractory anaphylaxis algorithm does not specifically recommend vasopressin; it lists IV adrenaline infusion titration, plus glucagon for beta-blocker patients. Vasopressin appears in some intensive-care rescue protocols but is not the RCH-cited next step.
flags.rch_says: RCH refractory anaphylaxis: titrate IV adrenaline infusion, ensure airway/breathing, fluids, consider glucagon if on beta-blockers, ICU input. Vasopressin not in RCH algorithm.
flags.proposed_fix: Either reframe correct option as 'PICU/anaesthetics review for advanced rescue; consider escalating adrenaline infusion and adding noradrenaline; vasopressin is a third-line ICU option' or add explicit RCH citation that this exceeds the published algorithm. Currently presents vasopressin as if it were RCH-standard.

flags.stem_snippet: 3yo failed cow's milk challenge with urticaria only - plan includes EpiPen Jr.
flags.issue: Correct option prescribes EpiPen Jr after a failed challenge with urticaria-only reaction. ASCIA (and by extension RCH) recommends adrenaline autoinjectors for confirmed anaphylaxis or high-risk food allergy with asthma; urticaria-only on a single challenge does not meet anaphylaxis criteria, and routine EpiPen prescription after non-anaphylactic reaction is debated.
flags.rch_says: Adrenaline autoinjectors are for patients at risk of anaphylaxis. Isolated urticaria without respiratory/CV involvement is not anaphylaxis.
flags.proposed_fix: Either upgrade reaction severity in stem (add wheeze or vomiting+wheeze) to justify AAI, or change correct option to 'ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions (non-anaphylaxis) and antihistamine, with AAI only if risk factors emerge'.

flags.stem_snippet: 5-week-old, fever, full and tense fontanelle (meningitis features).
flags.issue: Correct option = IV cefotaxime plus ampicillin at meningitic doses. This is the AAP/US regimen. The AU pattern (RCH Febrile child + Therapeutic Guidelines + AMH) for an infant 1-3 months with meningitic features is benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) PLUS gentamicin PLUS cefotaxime; pure cefotaxime+ampicillin omits the aminoglycoside which AU guidelines include for sepsis cover and Listeria/enterococcal coverage.
flags.rch_says: Empirical antibiotics for febrile infant 1-3 months guided by AU local protocols. RCH defers to Therapeutic Guidelines / local antimicrobial guidelines, which specify benzylpenicillin + gentamicin (+ cefotaxime if meningitis suspected) at this age.
flags.proposed_fix: Change correct option to 'IV benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin/ampicillin) PLUS gentamicin PLUS cefotaxime at meningitic doses' per AU/TG pattern. Add aciclovir if vesicles or focal seizure features per RCH HSV cover guidance.

flags.stem_snippet: 5-week-old, single fever 38.4, unsettled. Empirical regimen post-LP.
flags.issue: Correct option = 'IV ampicillin 50 mg/kg plus IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg'. This is the AAP/Boston-criteria regimen. AU pattern is benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) PLUS gentamicin (+/- cefotaxime if meningitis). Omitting gentamicin loses Gram-negative coverage that AU guidelines favour for this age band.
flags.rch_says: AU TG/AMH regimen for 1-3 mo febrile infant: benzylpenicillin + gentamicin; add cefotaxime if meningitis features. Cefotaxime + ampicillin alone is the US AAP regimen.
flags.proposed_fix: Change correct option to AU regimen 'IV benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin) PLUS gentamicin; add cefotaxime if meningitis suspected'. Keep current option as a US-derived distractor.

flags.stem_snippet: 6-week-old, BRUE-like event (pale, limp 20s during feed, now normal).
flags.issue: Correct option observes 4 hours then discharge. RCH BRUE pathway for low-risk BRUE supports brief monitored observation, but at 6 weeks RCH considers the infant higher-risk (age <2 months is a higher-risk feature in AAP and AU guidance). 4-hour discharge for 6-week-old BRUE may be too short per AU practice.
flags.rch_says: RCH does not have a dedicated BRUE guideline; AAP/Australian practice would admit infants <2 months for observation after BRUE rather than 4-hour discharge.
flags.proposed_fix: Lengthen observation to overnight admission given age <8 weeks, or add explicit low-risk BRUE criteria to stem to justify 4-hour discharge.
